DashboardNext Lesson

Preserving Values with useRef

I want to start this post off a little different than we usually do. That is, with a challenge. Given the code below, the goal being to create a StopWatch component that allows the user to start and stop the timer, can you spot the bug in it?

import * as React from "react"
import { formatTime } from "./utils"

export default function Stopwatch () {
const [seconds, setSeconds] = React.useState(0)
const [running, setRunning] = React.useState(false)
let id = null

const handleClick = () => {
if (running === false) {
id = window.setInterval(() => {
setSeconds(s => s + 1)
}, 1000)
setRunning(true)
} else {
window.clearInterval(id)
id = null
setRunning(false)
}
}

return (
<main>
<h1>{formatTime(seconds)}</h1>
<button
style={{background: running === true ? 'var(--red)' : 'var(--green)'}}
onClick={handleClick}>
{running === true ? 'Stop' : 'Start'}
</button>
</main>
)
}

Let's walk through it. We know in order to keep track of how much time has passed, we need the right combination of component state with a side effect. Because setInterval is a side effect that's triggered by an event, we've put it inside of an event handler.

When the user starts the timer, we start our interval, store its return value in an id variable so we can clear it later, and then we set running to true.

When the user stops the timer, we pass the id we stored to clearInterval, telling the browser to stop invoking our function every 1000 ms, and then set running to false.

Did you spot the bug? If not, .

If you try to stop the stopwatch, you'll notice that nothing happens. The bug is in how we're storing the id that is returned from our setInterval invocation. Right now, we're storing it as a normal JavaScript variable. What that means is that every time React re-renders, the value of id will be reset to null. Then, when the user stops the stopwatch, we're passing null to clearInterval which of course does nothing.

To fix this, instead of re-creating id on every render, what we want to do is tell React to preserve id across renders. Isn't this is the exact use case that useState was created for?

Let's try it. Instead of storing id as a normal Javascript variable, let's store it was as state using useState so we won't lose it when React re-renders.

import * as React from "react"
import { formatTime } from "./utils"

export default function Stopwatch () {
  const [seconds, setSeconds] = React.useState(0)
  const [running, setRunning] = React.useState(false)
  const [id, setId] = React.useState(null)

  const handleClick = () => {
    if (running === false) {
      const id = window.setInterval(() => {
        setSeconds(s => s + 1) 
      }, 1000)
      setId(id)
      setRunning(true)
    } else {
      window.clearInterval(id)
      setId(null)
      setRunning(false)
    }
  }

  return (
    <main>
      <h1>{formatTime(seconds)}</h1>
      <button
        style={{background: running === true ? 'var(--red)' : 'var(--green)'}}
        onClick={handleClick}>
          {running === true ? 'Stop' : 'Start'}
      </button>
    </main>
  )
}

That seems to have done the trick. Now when the user stops the stopwatch, we're passing the correct id to clearInterval because it doesn't get reset when our component re-renders.

So we're good, right? At this point in the course you probably know that if I ask that question, we are definitely not good.

Yes, our app technically does work. And I'm not even mad, more just disappointed. Throughout this whole course you've been taught, nauseatingly, that in React, your view is simply a function of your state.

As is, our app breaks this mental model. We've introduced state that has nothing to do with our view, we're simply using it as a way to preserve a value across renders. That's what we in the biz call hacky.

What we need is a way to tell React that we want to preserve a value across renders, but that value has nothing to do with the view, and therefore, React doesn't need to re-render when it changes.

Luckily for us, React provides an escape hatch for this exact scenario via a hook called useRef.

useRef

Creates a value that is preserved across renders, but won't trigger a re-render when it changes

The API is fairly simple. When you call useRef, what you get back is a ref – an object with a mutable current property whose value will be preserved across renders.

Like useState, you can specify the initial value of the ref's current property by passing an argument to useRef when you invoke it.

import * as React from "react"

export default function App () {
  const ref = React.useRef(null)

  console.log(ref)

  return <p>Check the console</p>
}

Unlike useState, this current property is and doesn't have a special mutation API. This means you can mutate it just like you'd mutate any other value, and doing so won't trigger a re-render.

import * as React from "react"

export default function App () {
  console.count('Rendering App')

  const ref = React.useRef(null)
  console.log(ref.current)

  ref.current = 1337
  console.log(ref.current)

  ref.current = { foo: "bar" }
  console.log(ref.current)

  return <p>Check the console</p>
}

Now we can use our knowledge of useRef to update our stopwatch example from earlier. We'll swap out our id state for a ref and use that to store the id that setInterval returns.

import * as React from "react"
import { formatTime } from "./utils"

export default function Stopwatch () {
  const [seconds, setSeconds] = React.useState(0)
  const [running, setRunning] = React.useState(false)
  const ref = React.useRef(null)

  const handleClick = () => {
    if (running === false) {
      ref.current = window.setInterval(() => {
        setSeconds(s => s + 1) 
      }, 1000)
      setRunning(true)
    } else {
      window.clearInterval(ref.current)
      setRunning(false)
    }
  }

  return (
    <main>
      <h1>{formatTime(seconds)}</h1>
      <button
        style={{background: running === true ? 'var(--red)' : 'var(--green)'}}
        onClick={handleClick}>
          {running === true ? 'Stop' : 'Start'}
      </button>
    </main>
  )
}

Much better. We no longer have any state that isn't used to update the view, and more importantly, we're potentially React some work since our app no longer re-renders when we store the id.

Let's look at another example.

Say we had an animation where CSS is doing all the heavy lifting and all we're doing with React is keeping track of the state of a toggle input. Something like this.

import * as React from "react"
import Diagram from "./Diagram"
import ReactLogo from "./ReactLogo"

export default function App() {
  const [active, setActive] = React.useState(false)

  const handleToggle = () => {
    setActive(!active)
  }

  return (
    <div
      className={`diagram ${active ? "react-approach" : "trad-approach"}`}
    >
      <Diagram />
      <div className="toggle-diagram">
        <input
          id="toggle"
          className="toggle-input"
          type="checkbox"
          checked={active}
          onChange={handleToggle}
        />
        <label htmlFor="toggle" className="toggle-label">
          <div
            className="toggle-options"
            data-checked="React"
            data-unchecked="Traditional"
          >
            <div className="toggle-switch">
              <span className="toggle-marker">
                <ReactLogo />
              </span>
            </div>
          </div>
        </label>
      </div>
    </div>
  )
}

You'll notice that our app is mostly fine, but there's one thing I think we can improve on. If you click on the toggle switch repeatedly, over and over, the experience breaks down. It still works, but I think the better UX is to only allow the user to toggle when the animation has finished running.

Because CSS is doing a lot of the heavy lifting, we don't have a ton of options for how to solve this. One approach that I think is good enough™ is to disable the toggle switch for a short period of time after it's been clicked – say 1300ms.

To do this, let's preserve a timestamp of the last time the toggle button was clicked, then, when it's clicked again, we'll only call setActive if more than 1300ms has passed.

Since this value we want to preserve has nothing to do with rendering the UI and we don't want React to re-render when it changes, we'll store it in a ref.

import * as React from "react"
import Diagram from "./Diagram"
import ReactLogo from "./ReactLogo"

export default function App() {
  const [active, setActive] = React.useState(false)
  const msRef = React.useRef(0)

  const handleToggle = () => {
    const now = Date.now()

    if (now - msRef.current > 1300) {
      setActive(!active)
      msRef.current = now
    }
  }

  return (
    <div
      className={`diagram ${active ? "react-approach" : "trad-approach"}`}
    >
      <Diagram />
      <div className="toggle-diagram">
        <input
          id="toggle"
          className="toggle-input"
          type="checkbox"
          checked={active}
          onChange={handleToggle}
        />
        <label htmlFor="toggle" className="toggle-label">
          <div
            className="toggle-options"
            data-checked="React"
            data-unchecked="Traditional"
          >
            <div className="toggle-switch">
              <span className="toggle-marker">
                <ReactLogo />
              </span>
            </div>
          </div>
        </label>
      </div>
    </div>
  )
}

Now, because we're using a ref to preserve the last time the button was clicked, we can use that to disable our toggle button without getting in React's way.

At this point in the course, if we were to summarize the mental model of React that we've built up, it would look something like this.

In React, your view is a function of your state – both of which you encapsulate inside of a component. To get your application, you compose all of your components together.

To get data down the component tree, you use props. To get data up the component tree, you use callbacks.

Sometimes you need to do things that don't fit into this regular React paradigm, these are called side effects and React provides some escape hatches for these scenarios.

If you have a side effect that is triggered by an event, put it in an event handler. If you have a side effect that is synchronizing your component with some outside system, put it inside of useEffect. If you need to preserve a value across renders, but that value has nothing to do with the view, and therefore, React doesn't need to re-render when it changes, put it in a ref using useRef.

So far, so good – almost.

There is just one more side effect that we haven't talked about yet, and it's crucial for building on the web – interacting directly with the DOM.

Now I know it seems weird, isn't the whole point of React to create an abstraction over the view (DOM) so you don't have to directly interact with it? Yes, mostly. But again, they're called escape hatches for a reason.

Take this scenario, for example.

Say we worked for a really hip marketing agency out of SOMA. We're tasked with making one of those toolbars that you can click and scroll to different sections of the page. Something like this (but one that actually works, scroll down manually).

import * as React from "react"
import Toolbar from "./Toolbar"

export default function App() {
  const handleClick = (type) => {
    console.log(type)
  }

  return (
    <div>
      <Toolbar handleClick={handleClick} />
      <div className="white" />
      <div className="yellow" />
      <div className="black" />
    </div>
  )
}

Knowing what you know about React, how would you go about solving this?

If you have some experience with DOM APIs, you might be familiar with the scrollIntoView method. It's a method that you can call on any DOM element and the browser will scroll that element into view... naturally.

element.scrollIntoView({
  behavior: "smooth",
  block: "nearest",
})

This would be perfect for us... if we had access to the element we wanted to scroll to.

One idea might be to say screw it and just use the DOM's getElementsByClassName method to manually grab the element we want to scroll to.

import * as React from "react"
import Toolbar from "./Toolbar"

export default function App() {
  const handleClick = (type) => {
    document.getElementsByClassName(type)[0].scrollIntoView({
      behavior: "smooth",
      block: "nearest",
    })
  }

  return (
    <div>
      <Toolbar handleClick={handleClick} />
      <div className="white" />
      <div className="yellow" />
      <div className="black" />
    </div>
  )
}

This actually works, but it's brittle, unnecessarily dependant on string matching, and not very React-y. In fact, it kind of feels like we're giving React the 🖕 since we're bypassing it directly and going straight to the DOM.

Instead of needing to query the DOM directly, React comes with an escape hatch that allows us to get a reference to any DOM element that it creates – and it does so by utilizing what we learned about earlier, refs.

Whenever you pass a ref as a prop to a React element, React will put a reference to the DOM node it creates into that ref's current property.

import * as React from "react"

export default function App () {
  const ref = React.useRef(null)

  const handleClick = () => {
    const node = ref.current
    node.classList.add("🖕-you-react")
    node.innerHTML = "Suck it, React"
  }

  return (
    <button ref={ref} onClick={handleClick}>
      Click Me
    </button>
  )
}

Of course, manually manipulating the DOM like this is a terrible idea since that's React's job and React will have no insight into the changes you make... but it does make for a great representation for how DOM refs work.

Now that we know how to use refs to get access to DOM nodes, let's update our scrolling example from earlier.

We'll create three refs, one for each of our sections. We'll then attach those to their own DOM element and call scrollIntoView on them when they're clicked.

import * as React from "react"
import Toolbar from "./Toolbar"

export default function App() {
  const white = React.useRef(null)
  const black = React.useRef(null)
  const yellow = React.useRef(null)

  const handleClick = (type) => {
    let ref = null

    if (type === "white") ref = white
    if (type === "yellow") ref = yellow
    if (type === "black") ref = black

    ref.current.scrollIntoView({
      behavior: "smooth",
      block: "nearest",
    })
  }

  return (
    <div>
      <Toolbar handleClick={handleClick} />
      <div ref={white} className="white" />
      <div ref={yellow} className="yellow" />
      <div ref={black} className="black" />
    </div>
  )
}

Much better. Now we have complete control over which elements we scroll to and we're no longer dependant on class names or the layout of the DOM. Again, this works because when you pass a ref as a prop to a React element, React will put a reference to the DOM node it creates into that ref's current property.

Now, there is one thing that's been eating me up since earlier. It was this line when we were discussing how to disable our toggle button until after all our CSS animations had finished - "One approach that I think is good enough™ is to disable the toggle switch for a short period of time after it's been clicked – say 1300ms."

This worked, but it too was brittle. If a designer tweaked how long our animations took to run, it wouldn't behave as expected.

At the time, all we knew about useRef was that it allowed us to preserve a value across renders, without triggering a re-render when it changed. What we didn't know was how we could use it to get access to DOM nodes – and specifically in our example, determine when all CSS animations had finished for a particular DOM node.

Let's do some refactoring. As a reminder, this was where we left off.

import * as React from "react"
import Diagram from "./Diagram"
import ReactLogo from "./ReactLogo"

export default function App() {
  const [active, setActive] = React.useState(false)
  const msRef = React.useRef(0)

  const handleToggle = () => {
    const now = Date.now()

    if (now - msRef.current > 1300) {
      setActive(!active)
      msRef.current = now
    }
  }

  return (
    <div
      className={`diagram ${active ? "react-approach" : "trad-approach"}`}
    >
      <Diagram />
      <div className="toggle-diagram">
        <input
          id="toggle"
          className="toggle-input"
          type="checkbox"
          checked={active}
          onChange={handleToggle}
        />
        <label htmlFor="toggle" className="toggle-label">
          <div
            className="toggle-options"
            data-checked="React"
            data-unchecked="Traditional"
          >
            <div className="toggle-switch">
              <span className="toggle-marker">
                <ReactLogo />
              </span>
            </div>
          </div>
        </label>
      </div>
    </div>
  )
}
🐲

Here be dragons

Before we go on, I want to say that I would consider everything below this to be advanced. Don't get down on yourself if it's a little confusing at first. I've been writing React since 2014 and it even took me a little while to get a solution I was happy with.

With that said, it's a fantastic example of using everything we've learned up until this point in the course to properly utilize escape hatches in React.

Let's start by getting the easy parts out of the way.

Let's get rid of our old code for keeping track of the last time the toggle button was clicked and create two new refs. One to get access to the root div element, so we can use the browsers's getAnimations method on it. The other to keep track of if the CSS animations are still running, so we can avoid calling setActive if they are.

import * as React from "react"
import Diagram from "./Diagram"
import ReactLogo from "./ReactLogo"

export default function App() {
  const [active, setActive] = React.useState(false)
  const node = React.useRef(null)
  const animating = React.useRef(false)

  const handleToggle = () => {
    if (animating.current === false) {
      setActive(!active)
    }
  }

  return (
    <div
      ref={node}
      className={`diagram ${active ? "react-approach" : "trad-approach"}`}
    >
      <Diagram />
      <div className="toggle-diagram">
        <input
          id="toggle"
          className="toggle-input"
          type="checkbox"
          checked={active}
          onChange={handleToggle}
        />
        <label htmlFor="toggle" className="toggle-label">
          <div
            className="toggle-options"
            data-checked="React"
            data-unchecked="Traditional"
          >
            <div className="toggle-switch">
              <span className="toggle-marker">
                <ReactLogo />
              </span>
            </div>
          </div>
        </label>
      </div>
    </div>
  )
}

Now the hard part, figuring out if we have any CSS animations running and keeping our animating ref up to date with that info.

In a way, what we're really trying to do is synchronize the current state of our CSS animations with our component. This, by definition, is the perfect use case for useEffect. To do this, inside of our effect, we'll use the browser's getAnimations API to get all the animations in our entire subtree.

React.useEffect(() => {
  const checkAnimations = async () => {
    const animations = node.current.getAnimations({ subtree: true })


  }


  checkAnimations()
})

Next, we'll map over all of our animations, creating an array of promises that will each resolve once its animation has finished running.

React.useEffect(() => {
  const checkAnimations = async () => {
    const animations = node.current.getAnimations({ subtree: true })
    const promises = animations.map((animation) => animation.finished)


  }


  checkAnimations()
})

Then, once we have our array of promises, we can use Promise.all to wait for all of them to resolve – which they will once all the animations have finished running.

React.useEffect(() => {
  const checkAnimations = async () => {
    const animations = node.current.getAnimations({ subtree: true })
    const promises = animations.map((animation) => animation.finished)
    await Promise.all(promises)


  }


  checkAnimations()
})

Finally, the whole point of this, we want to update our animating ref both at the beginning and end of this process to keep it in sync with the current state of our animations.

import * as React from "react"
import Diagram from "./Diagram"
import ReactLogo from "./ReactLogo"

export default function App() {
  const [active, setActive] = React.useState(false)
  const node = React.useRef(null)
  const animating = React.useRef(false)

  React.useEffect(() => {
    const checkAnimations = async () => {
      animating.current = true;
      const animations = node.current.getAnimations({ subtree: true })
      const promises = animations.map((animation) => animation.finished)
      await Promise.all(promises)
      animating.current = false;
    }

    checkAnimations()
  })

  const handleToggle = () => {
    if (animating.current === false) {
      setActive(!active)
    }
  }

  return (
    <div
      ref={node}
      className={`diagram ${active ? "react-approach" : "trad-approach"}`}
    >
      <Diagram />
      <div className="toggle-diagram">
        <input
          id="toggle"
          className="toggle-input"
          type="checkbox"
          checked={active}
          onChange={handleToggle}
        />
        <label htmlFor="toggle" className="toggle-label">
          <div
            className="toggle-options"
            data-checked="React"
            data-unchecked="Traditional"
          >
            <div className="toggle-switch">
              <span className="toggle-marker">
                <ReactLogo />
              </span>
            </div>
          </div>
        </label>
      </div>
    </div>
  )
}

Much better. Now, instead of disabling our toggle switch for an arbitrary amount of time, we only disable it for the exact amount of time that it takes for all our animations to finish running.

One thing you may have noticed is that our useEffect doesn't have a dependency array. This makes sense as our effect doesn't have any dependencies and we want to synchronize after every render, not just the initial one.

To recap, we learned about useRef, a hook that allows you to create a value that is preserved across renders, but won't trigger a re-render when it changes. We also learned that if we pass a ref as a prop to a React element, React will put a reference to the DOM node it creates into that ref's current property.

just to clarify, useEffect does not need the "active" state in the dependency array, because upon clicking the button and calling handleClick, it calls setActive(!active), which changes the state, which triggers a rerender, which causes useEffect to run again, which calls the checkAnimations() function. Therefore no need to have a dependency array. Am I thinking about this correctly?

Masrur Mahmood
3 months ago

Now that we know the uses of useRef, I had a question about the "Hacker News" challenge. The accepted solution for the useEffect() to keep track to ignore the network requests. Couldn't we use useRef() to keep track of the ignore variable OR keep track of all the network calls and await on the promises?

React.useEffect(() => {
    // use the ignore
    let ignore = false;

    // first perform the async function
    const handleFetchResults = async () => {
        // we show the loading animation by setting the setLoader() set
        setLoading(true);
        // we set the results also to an empty array
        setResults([]);
        
        // we retrieve the results and list of pages from the async function
        // passing in the states - query page and tag
        const { results, pages } = await fetchData({query, page, tag})
        
        // if the ignore was trigged to true by another network call returning
        // first, we should return right away
        if (ignore) return;
        
        // we set the results from fetchData return values
        setResults(results);
        // we set the total pages from the fetchData return values
        setTotalPages(pages);
        // reset loading
        setLoading(false);
    }
    
    // run to fetch results
    handleFetchResults();
    
    // return an ignore of true inside a closure
    return () => {
        ignore = true;
    }
}, [query, page, tag]);

I am a bit confused about the rules and how they interact.

Rule 1 says that we should use an event handler to maintain any side effects. Rule 2 says that we should use React.useEffect anytime we synchronize with an external system.
